# UC-13.1.2 · Search Concurrency Monitoring

## Description

Exceeding search concurrency limits causes search skipping and degraded user experience. Monitoring guides capacity decisions.

## Value

Exceeding search concurrency limits causes search skipping and degraded user experience. Monitoring guides capacity decisions.

## Implementation

Track concurrent searches vs configured limits. Alert when approaching concurrency limits. Identify resource-intensive searches consuming disproportionate capacity. Report on search workload distribution.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Monitoring Console.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `_internal` (scheduler logs, search dispatch).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track concurrent searches vs configured limits. Alert when approaching concurrency limits. Identify resource-intensive searches consuming disproportionate capacity. Report on search workload distribution.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd group=search_concurrency
| timechart span=5m max(active_hist_searches) as historical, max(active_rt_searches) as realtime
```

Understanding this SPL

**Search Concurrency Monitoring** — Exceeding search concurrency limits causes search skipping and degraded user experience. Monitoring guides capacity decisions.

Documented **Data sources**: `_internal` (scheduler logs, search dispatch). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Monitoring Console. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal; **sourcetype**: splunkd. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_internal, sourcetype=splunkd. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=5m** buckets — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search over calm and busy windows. Compare with the Splunk monitoring console and relevant health and introspection views, and spot-check key fields in sample events.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (concurrent searches over time), Gauge (% of limit), Table (top resource consumers).

## SPL

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd group=search_concurrency
| timechart span=5m max(active_hist_searches) as historical, max(active_rt_searches) as realtime
```

## Visualization

Line chart (concurrent searches over time), Gauge (% of limit), Table (top resource consumers).
